title: SEGMENT 14 — Nanopore direct methylation calling — methylation as a bonus output

lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — Nanopore direct methylation calling — methylation as a bonus output
There is a completely different way to read methylation that doesn't involve chemistry at all.

Oxford Nanopore Technologies' platform — which sequences DNA by threading individual molecules through tiny protein pores embedded in a membrane, while measuring the ionic current that flows through the pore as each base passes — turns out to be directly sensitive to base modifications.
A methylated cytosine perturbs the ionic current differently from an unmethylated cytosine; a 5hmC perturbs it differently again.
The current signal carries the methylation information, embedded in the same data stream that carries the sequence information.
For the first several years of nanopore sequencing, this signal was a curiosity.
The basecaller — the software that converts raw current data into sequence reads — was tuned to call A, C, G, and T, and it ignored modifications.

But as the basecallers got more sophisticated, particularly with the introduction of neural-network-based basecalling around 2018, it became possible to train the same models to recognize and call modifications directly from the current signal.
The modern Oxford Nanopore basecaller — the Dorado software — outputs methylation calls alongside base calls, with no additional sample preparation, no chemistry, no separate experiment.
Here is the analogy.
Nanopore methylation calling is the bonus output.
The same long-read nanopore sequencing that gives you sequence also reports methylation status from the raw current signal, no extra chemistry needed.
You do one library prep, one sequencing run, and the basecaller gives you sequence plus methylation in the same file.
It's the methylation method that bundles with every nanopore experiment for free.
The implications are substantial.
First, you avoid all the bisulfite/EM-seq/TAPS chemistry entirely.
No conversion, no damage, no library doubling for 5mC versus 5hmC discrimination.
Second, you get long reads.
Standard nanopore reads are tens of thousands of base pairs; ultra-long reads can hit a million base pairs.

This means you can phase methylation across long genomic distances — for example, telling you whether two methylated CpGs 50 kilobases apart are on the same DNA molecule or on different molecules, which matters for imprinting analysis, allele-specific methylation, and tumor clonality.
Third, you can call multiple modifications.
Nanopore basecallers can now distinguish 5mC from 5hmC from 6mA (a different modification, important in some bacteria and in some unusual human contexts) — all from the same sequencing run.
The trade-offs are real too.
Per-base accuracy of nanopore methylation calls, even in the best modern implementations, is somewhat lower than the accuracy of bisulfite or EM-seq calls at high coverage.
To get reliable methylation calls at a given CpG, you typically need higher coverage from nanopore than from short-read converted sequencing — maybe 20x to 30x nanopore versus 5x to 10x bisulfite.
The cost per base for nanopore is still higher than for Illumina-based methods, though it's dropping fast.

And the basecaller's modification-calling accuracy is sensitive to the sequence context, the basecaller version, and the chemistry of the flow cell, so reproducibility across versions and labs requires careful version control.
PacBio HiFi sequencing, the other long-read platform, also has methylation calling capabilities, derived from polymerase kinetics rather than ionic current.

The signal is subtler — PacBio infers methylation from how the polymerase pauses or accelerates at modified bases — and the current implementations primarily call 5mC at CpG sites with somewhat narrower scope than nanopore, but they're improving.
By the late 2020s, both long-read platforms will have full methylation-calling capability built into the standard data pipeline.

For applications that need long reads and methylation together — particularly anything involving repetitive regions of the genome, structural variants, imprinted loci, or haplotype-phased methylation — nanopore methylation calling is increasingly the method of choice.

For high-throughput, low-cost, large-cohort methylation studies, the EPIC array and EM-seq will probably dominate for some years yet, because the per-sample cost of nanopore is still too high for studies of thousands of samples.
But the trajectory is clear: methylation will increasingly be a free side effect of doing long-read genome sequencing, rather than a separate experiment requiring its own chemistry.
The long-term implication is that the entire bisulfite-based methylation industry may eventually shrink as long-read sequencing becomes cheap enough to be the default.
That transition is happening now, slowly, and we'll see it play out over the next decade.
